statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, find the smallest divisor that can divide all elements in the array without leaving a remainder. The divisor should be greater than 1. If no such divisor exists, return -1. The array will contain at least two elements and at most 10000 elements. Each element will be between 2 and 10000.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer N, the number of elements in the array. The second line contains N space-separated integers, the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: A single integer, the smallest divisor that can divide all elements in the array without leaving a remainder. If no such divisor exists, return -1.

constraints.description: Number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: The smallest divisor that can divide all elements in the array is 6, since 24 = 6 * 4, 30 = 6 * 5, 36 = 6 * 6, and 48 = 6 * 8.
No divisor greater than 1 can divide all elements in the array without leaving a remainder, so the function returns -1.

hints.text: Start by finding the greatest common divisor (GCD) of all elements in the array. The GCD is a number that divides all elements in the array without leaving a remainder.

You can use the Euclidean algorithm to find the GCD of two numbers. Apply this algorithm to find the GCD of the first two elements in the array, then apply it to the result and the next element, and so on.

Notice that the GCD of all elements in the array will be the smallest divisor that can divide all elements without leaving a remainder. However, the problem statement requires the divisor to be greater than 1. So, you need to check if the GCD is greater than 1 before returning it.
If the GCD is not greater than 1, it means that there is no common divisor greater than 1 that can divide all elements in the array without leaving a remainder. In this case, return -1.

editorial.title: Finding the Smallest Divisor
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the smallest divisor of the greatest common divisor (GCD) of all numbers in the given array. Initially, one might consider a brute force approach, checking every possible divisor for each number, but this is inefficient due to its high time complexity.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force solution would involve checking every number up to the maximum value in the array to see if it is a divisor of all numbers. However, this approach is impractical due to its time complexity of O(n*m), where n is the number of elements and m is the maximum value in the array.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient approach involves finding the GCD of all numbers in the array first, then checking for divisors of the GCD. This reduces the search space significantly.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution calculates the GCD of all numbers in the array using the Euclidean algorithm, which has a time complexity of O(log(m)), where m is the maximum value in the array. Then, it iterates from 2 to the GCD to find the smallest divisor, resulting in an overall time complexity of O(n * log(m)) and a space complexity of O(1).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that the GCD of all numbers in the array is the largest number that divides all numbers without a remainder. Then, the smallest divisor of the GCD is the smallest number that divides the GCD without a remainder, which is also a divisor of all numbers in the array.
editorial.dryRun: To verify the solution, one can perform a dry run with sample inputs to ensure that the GCD is calculated correctly and the smallest divisor is found as expected.
commonMistakes: Not considering the GCD of all numbers in the array
Using a brute force approach to find divisors
Not handling the case where the GCD is a prime number
Incorrectly implementing the Euclidean algorithm for GCD calculation
Not checking for divisors starting from 2
